AgentTaskGraph stays a flat dict[str, AgentTask]. The tasks: YAML key is now optional (a YAML with only tdd_groups: is valid).
  • Cross-group dependencies: a dep string that matches another group's id resolves to {dep}_impl at build time; plain task IDs pass through unchanged.
  • Role-specific prompts: _build_task_prompt() in run_graph.py dispatches to _build_test_writer_prompt, _build_test_reviewer_prompt, _build_implementer_prompt, or _build_generic_prompt based on task.role. Reviewer writes {task.id}.md (e.g. stats_module_review.md); implementer derives the review filename from task.id.
  • New test coverage: new test/test_run_graph.py; additions to test_agent_task.py and test_agent_task_graph.py — 171 tests total.

Key files: agent_task.py, agent_task_graph.py, run_graph.py.

docs.title: BACKLOG.md for quick idea capture — PR #25
docs.text: Added docs/BACKLOG.md — a lightweight place to park ideas immediately without interrupting a current task. Three sections: Features, Improvements, Ideas/Maybe. Items graduate to HISTORY.md when done. Added a pointer in CLAUDE.md so new sessions see it.

docs.title: Black + isort formatting pass — PR #24
docs.text: Ran pixi run check (introduced in PR #23) across the whole codebase to apply black line wrapping, blank-line normalization, and isort import ordering. No functional changes — pure formatting.

docs.title: CLAUDE.md, OPERATIONS.md, HISTORY.md, pixi run check — PR #23
docs.text:
Three usability improvements landed together:

  • CLAUDE.md at repo root: concise project reference auto-loaded by Claude Code at session start — commands, module map, signal files, YAML keys, and links to detailed docs.
  • pixi run check: one command for format + typecheck + test — the canonical pre-PR verification step.
  • docs/OPERATIONS.md: day-to-day running guide (running a graph, reading results, resetting after a run, common failure modes).
  • docs/HISTORY.md: per-PR development log covering all 23 PRs to that point.

docs.title: Agent PR body capture + summary.md — PR #22
docs.text:
Agents are now prompted to write a real ## Summary / ## Files changed PR body instead of the placeholder "Automated task.". Before merging, the orchestrator fetches the body via gh pr view --json body and writes it to .workflow/<graph>/signals/<task-id>/summary.md. The final status report shows the summary path for each completed task.

Key functions: save_pr_summary() in task_launcher.py.

docs.title: Configurable tmux session + keep_panes + graph reset — PR #21
docs.text:
Three related usability improvements:

  • tmux_session graph YAML field + --tmux-session CLI flag: controls which tmux session agent windows open in (default: "agentrelay").
  • keep_panes graph YAML field + --keep-panes CLI flag: suppresses auto-close of agent tmux windows after task completion (useful for debugging).
  • reset_graph.py module + record_run_start(): python -m agentrelay.reset_graph reads run_info.json (written at graph start) and fully resets the target repo — closes open PRs, git reset --hard + force-push, deletes remote branches, removes worktrees and signal dirs.

docs.title: pixi.lock neutralize-and-regenerate — PR #20
docs.text:
Eliminates pixi.lock merge conflicts when parallel agents both modify pixi.toml. Before merging any PR, the orchestrator restores main's current pixi.lock into the agent branch (neutralizing the agent's version), then after merging runs pixi install and commits the freshly resolved lock file to main.

Key functions: neutralize_pixi_lock_in_pr(), commit_pixi_lock_to_main().

docs.title: pixi.toml change detection — PR #19
docs.text:
After merging a PR, the orchestrator checks whether pixi.toml changed (via gh pr view --json files). If so, it runs pixi install on the target repo to keep the environment in sync.

Key functions: pixi_toml_changed_in_pr(), run_pixi_install().

docs.title: Agent log capture + sibling repo support + target_repo_root rename — PR #18
docs.text:
• Agent log capture: after each task, the orchestrator captures the full tmux pane scrollback and writes it to .workflow/<graph>/signals/<task-id>/agent.log.
  • Sibling repo support: AgentTaskGraphBuilder.from_yaml() accepts a target_repo_root override, enabling the orchestrator to drive tasks in a separate sibling repository (e.g. agentrelaydemos).
  • Rename: repo_root → target_repo_root throughout for clarity.

docs.title: Bypass --dangerously-skip-permissions dialog — PR #17
docs.text: Fixed the send_prompt() timing sequence to correctly navigate the Claude Code confirmation dialog (Down + Enter) before sending the task prompt.

docs.title: Multi-task async graph dispatch loop — PR #14
docs.text: Core orchestrator loop (run_graph.py): loads a YAML graph, resolves task dependencies, dispatches ready tasks concurrently via asyncio, polls for completion signals, and merges PRs in order. Supports resuming interrupted runs (signals persist across restarts).

docs.title: pull_main after merge — PR #11
docs.text: After merging a task PR, the orchestrator fast-forwards the local main branch (git pull --ff-only) so subsequent tasks branch from the correct HEAD.

docs.title: .workflow/ gitignored; graph YAML convention — PR #12
docs.text: All runtime state (.workflow/) is gitignored. Graph definitions (graphs/) are version-controlled. This keeps the working tree clean and makes graph definitions reviewable and reusable.

docs.title: PR workflow + timing fixes — PR #5
docs.text: Agents create their own PRs and write the URL into the .done signal file. Fixed timing delays in send_prompt() to ensure Claude finishes initializing before the task prompt is sent.

docs.title: PATH propagation in launch_agent — PR #7
docs.text: Exports the orchestrator's PATH into each agent's tmux environment so tools like gh, pixi, and claude are available in agent bash subshells.

docs.title: Foundation — PRs #1–#3
docs.text:
• Project scaffolding: pyproject.toml, pixi.toml, source layout
  • Documentation: PROJECT_DESCRIPTION.md, WORKFLOW_DESCRIPTION.md, DESIGN_DECISIONS.md, REPO_SETUP.md
  • Core infrastructure: AgentTask, AgentTaskGraph, AgentTaskGraphBuilder, WorktreeTaskRunner, task_launcher functions, demo script, initial tests

docs.title: Operations Guide
docs.text: Day-to-day procedures for running and managing v01 agentrelay graphs.

docs.title: Running a graph
docs.text:
# From this repository root
pixi run python -m agentrelay.prototypes.v01.run_graph graphs/<name>.yaml

# Override tmux session
pixi run python -m agentrelay.prototypes.v01.run_graph graphs/<name>.yaml --tmux-session <session>

# Keep agent tmux windows open after tasks complete
pixi run python -m agentrelay.prototypes.v01.run_graph graphs/<name>.yaml --keep-panes

The graph YAML itself can also set tmux_session: and keep_panes: defaults.

docs.title: Graph YAML quick reference
docs.text:
name: my-graph
target_repo: /path/to/target/repo
tmux_session: agentrelay   # optional; default "agentrelay"
keep_panes: false          # optional

tasks:
  - id: setup
    description: "Initial project scaffolding"
    dependencies: []
  - id: stats_module_tests
    description: >-
      Create src/pkg/stats.py with mean() and median() functions.
      Both raise ValueError on empty input.
      Tests go in tests/test_stats.py.
    role: test_writer
    dependencies: ["setup"]
  - id: stats_module_review
    description: "Review tests for stats module"
    role: test_reviewer
    dependencies: ["stats_module_tests"]
  - id: stats_module_impl
    description: "Implement stats module"
    role: implementer
    dependencies: ["stats_module_review"]

Each task gets its own worktree and PR.

docs.title: Reading results
docs.text:
After a run, task artifacts are written under:

<target_repo>/.workflow/<graph>/signals/<task-id>/
File Contents .done Timestamp (line 1) + PR URL (line 2) .merged Timestamp of successful merge agent.log Full tmux pane scrollback summary.md Agent PR body (if available)

To view a summary:

cat .workflow/<graph>/signals/<task-id>/summary.md

To watch live output:

tmux attach -t agentrelay

docs.title: Resetting after a run
docs.text:
The reset script returns the target repo to the start_head recorded in .workflow/<graph>/run_info.json.

pixi run python -m agentrelay.prototypes.v01.reset_graph graphs/<name>.yaml
pixi run python -m agentrelay.prototypes.v01.reset_graph graphs/<name>.yaml --yes

Reset performs:

  1. Close open PRs on task/<graph>/* and graph/<graph> branches
  2. Reset main back to start_head and push with --force-with-lease (unless out-of-order reset is detected)
  3. Delete remote task/graph branches
  4. Remove local worktrees under worktrees/<graph>/
  5. Remove .workflow/<graph>/

docs.title: Common failure modes
docs.text:
Agent window opens in wrong tmux session Use --tmux-session or set tmux_session: in YAML.

Pane closes before inspection Use --keep-panes.

Task appears stuck Inspect agent.log; if needed, write .failed manually in that task's signal directory.

PR merge not mergeable Commonly a lockfile/content conflict. Review PR diff and rerun/reset as needed.

Reset rejected (--force-with-lease) New commits landed on origin/main since run start; resolve manually before retrying reset.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The v01 prototype has two roles:

  • Orchestrator: Python process that loads a graph, dispatches agents, and merges PRs
  • Worktree agent: Claude Code process launched in an isolated worktree for one task

Coordination uses files in .workflow/<graph-name>/signals/<task-id>/.

docs.title: Directory Layout
docs.text:
<repo-root>/
  .workflow/
    <graph-name>/
      run_info.json
      signals/
        <task-id>/
          .done
          .merged
          .failed
          agent.log
          summary.md

<worktrees-root>/
  <graph-name>/
    <task-id>/

Git branches follow task/<graph-name>/<task-id>.

docs.title: Orchestrator Loop (v01)
docs.text:
1. Load graph YAML via AgentTaskGraphBuilder
  2. Hydrate completed/failed status from signal files
  3. Mark dependency-satisfied tasks as READY
  4. For each ready task:
  5. create worktree + branch
  6. write task context
  7. launch Claude in tmux
  8. send role-specific instructions
  9. Poll for .done or .failed
  10. On .done, merge PR and write .merged
  11. Remove worktree and refresh readiness

The loop runs with asyncio and dispatches ready tasks concurrently.

docs.title: Task Roles
docs.text:
v01 supports role-specific prompt templates:

  • GENERIC
  • SPEC_WRITER
  • TEST_WRITER
  • TEST_REVIEWER
  • IMPLEMENTER
  • MERGER

A common TDD chain is:

<feature>_tests -> <feature>_review -> <feature>_impl

Each task is a separate worktree + PR cycle.

docs.title: Signal Ownership
docs.text: Signal Writer Meaning .done Agent Task finished; includes PR URL .failed Agent Task failed; includes reason .merged Orchestrator PR merged agent.log Orchestrator Pane transcript summary.md Orchestrator PR body snapshot

docs.title: Tmux Targeting Note
docs.text: Automation should target tmux panes by pane_id (for example %3), not by window index. Window indices can renumber as windows close; pane_id remains stable.

docs.title: Resume Behavior
docs.text: Signal directories are intentionally durable. Re-running a graph hydrates state from existing signals. For a fresh run, delete .workflow/<graph-name>/ first.

docs.title: Review Findings (ordered by severity)
docs.text:
1. Critical: Tasks can be marked DONE without any merge if .done lacks a PR URL. In _run_task, task.state.status = DONE is set even when pr_url is empty, so no merge or .merged signal occurs. This can unblock dependents with missing code. Reference: run_graph.py:747, run_graph.py:779, run_graph.py:829

  2. Critical: If graph branch refresh fails after merge, downstream tasks can still start from stale code. pull_graph_branch() failure only logs a warning, but task is still marked DONE. Since new worktrees are based on local graph/<name>, dependents may run from outdated branch state. Reference: run_graph.py:820, run_graph.py:829, task_launcher.py:78

  3. High: create_graph_branch() can hard-reset working tree unexpectedly. Fallback path runs git reset --hard <base_branch> if git branch -f fails, which can discard local changes in target repo. Reference: task_launcher.py:50, task_launcher.py:57

  4. High: Resume behavior for .done is not implemented (doc/code mismatch). Docs say .done without .merged should attempt merge on startup; code only hydrates from .merged/.failed. Reference: agent_task_graph.py:80, WORKFLOW_DESCRIPTION.md:60

  5. Medium: Dependency graph validation is incomplete. _topo_sort doesn’t detect cycles or unknown deps explicitly, which can produce silent/incomplete scheduling or unclear errors. Reference: agent_task_graph.py:90

  6. Medium: Packaging metadata is incomplete for runtime install outside Pixi. pyproject.toml has empty dependencies, but runtime imports yaml. Reference: pyproject.toml:3, agent_task_graph.py:7

  7. Medium (architecture): Assistant backend is tightly coupled to Claude+tmux. claude, --dangerously-skip-permissions, tmux lifecycle, and Claude-TUI readiness checks are hardcoded, making Open/Closed and DIP weak for backend expansion (Copilot/Codex/Gemini). Reference: task_launcher.py:176, task_launcher.py:183, task_launcher.py:719, docs/BACKLOG.md:32

docs.title: SOLID-oriented Cleanup Opportunities (for pluggable assistants)
docs.text:
1. Split orchestration into ports/adapters: AgentHarness, RepoOps, PullRequestOps, SignalStore, InstructionBuilder.
  2. Keep current behavior behind adapters first: ClaudeTmuxHarness, GitCliRepoOps, GhCliPullRequestOps, FileSignalStore.
  3. Add backend selection at graph/task level: default backend at graph level; task override for routing.
  4. Make orchestrator state explicit: use durable run_state.json with states like WAITING_AGENT, WAITING_MERGE, DONE.
  5. Refactor task_launcher.py (1105 LOC) by responsibility: split into git_ops.py, gh_ops.py, tmux_harness.py, signals.py.
  6. Add contract tests for each port and one fake end-to-end orchestrator test.

docs.title: Testing status
docs.text:
• pixi run pytest -q: 375 passed.

  • Plain pytest -q in non-pixi shell fails due environment/package setup mismatch.

  • If you want, I can implement the first refactor slice now: introduce AgentHarness interface and move current Claude/tmux logic behind ClaudeTmuxHarness without changing behavior.

  • I can also patch the two critical correctness issues first (DONE without merge, stale-branch progression) before structural refactor.
  • If preferred, I can draft the YAML/schema changes for backend and adapter registry next.

docs.title: Architectural Review: SOLID Analysis & Cleanup Opportunities
Executive Summary
docs.text: The codebase (~2,800 LOC across 7 production files) is well-structured for its current size — clean data models, good test coverage, and sensible conventions. But two files dominate complexity (task_launcher.py at 1,105 lines and run_graph.py at 952 lines), and the code is tightly coupled to specific external tools (Claude CLI, tmux, git+GitHub). The backlog goal of making the agent backend pluggable requires addressing these structural issues first.

docs.title: 1. SRP: task_launcher.py is a god module
docs.text:
task_launcher.py has at least 6 distinct responsibilities in one flat collection of 30+ functions:

Concern Example functions Lines (approx) Git worktree/branch ops create_worktree, remove_worktree, create_graph_branch, pull_main, list_remote_task_branches, reset_target_repo_to_head ~250 Tmux window management launch_agent, send_prompt, _wait_for_claude_tui, save_agent_log, close_agent_pane ~170 GitHub PR operations create_final_pr, merge_pr, pixi_toml_changed_in_pr, save_pr_summary, append_concerns_to_pr ~200 Signal file I/O write_task_context, write_context, write_instructions, write_merged_signal, read_done_note, poll_for_completion ~200 ADR scanning scan_adr_section, write_adr_index_to_graph_branch, _extract_front_matter_field ~170 Run tracking + gates record_run_start, read_run_info, run_completion_gate, record_gate_failure, neutralize_pixi_lock_in_pr, run_pixi_install ~120

Recommendation: Split into focused modules — git_ops.py, terminal_ops.py, github_ops.py, signal_io.py, adr_ops.py. Each module gets its own responsibility and can evolve independently.

docs.title: 2. SRP: run_graph.py mixes orchestration with prompt generation
docs.text:
run_graph.py is ~950 lines, roughly half of which are prompt template strings embedded in Python code:

  • _build_spec_writer_prompt (~75 lines)
  • _build_test_writer_prompt (~55 lines)
  • _build_test_reviewer_prompt (~50 lines)
  • _build_implementer_prompt (~75 lines)
  • _build_generic_instructions (~100 lines)
  • _build_merger_prompt (~55 lines)

Plus helpers: _resolve_gate, _build_context_content, _adr_step, _spec_reading_step, _effective_verbosity.

Recommendation: Extract to a prompt_builder.py module. The orchestrator loop (_run_task, _run_graph_loop, main) stays in run_graph.py as pure coordination logic. Prompt-building is already tested independently in test_run_graph.py.

docs.title: 3. OCP + DIP: No abstraction boundary for the agent backend
docs.text:
This is the critical issue for the pluggability goal. The agent launch sequence is hardcoded to Claude CLI + tmux across multiple functions:

  • task_launcher.py launch_agent() — hardcodes tmux new-window + claude --dangerously-skip-permissions
  • _wait_for_claude_tui() — polls for "bypass permissions" text specific to Claude TUI
  • send_prompt() — uses tmux send-keys
  • save_agent_log() — uses tmux capture-pane
  • close_agent_pane() — uses tmux kill-window

To support Copilot/Codex/Gemini, a protocol-based abstraction is needed:

class AgentHarness(Protocol):
    """Abstraction over how a coding assistant is launched and communicated with."""

    def launch(self, task_id: str, cwd: Path, model: str | None,
               signal_dir: Path, session_name: str) -> str:
        """Launch an agent. Returns an opaque handle for later operations."""
        ...

    def send_prompt(self, handle: str, prompt: str) -> None: ...

    def capture_log(self, handle: str) -> str: ...

    def close(self, handle: str) -> None: ...

    def is_ready(self, handle: str, timeout: float) -> bool: ...

ClaudeCodeTmuxHarness implements this with the current tmux+claude logic. A future CopilotHarness or CodexHarness implements the same interface with different mechanics.

docs.title: 4. DIP: Orchestrator depends on concrete implementations
docs.text:
run_graph.py imports 26 concrete functions from task_launcher at the top of the file. Any change to how git/tmux/GitHub works requires touching both the launcher and the orchestrator. The orchestrator should depend on abstractions, not a bag of 26 free functions.

Recommendation: Group operations behind protocol-based service objects injected into the orchestrator:

@dataclass
class Orchestrator:
    graph: AgentTaskGraph
    harness: AgentHarness          # agent launch/communication
    vcs: VCSOperations             # git worktree/branch ops
    code_host: CodeHostOperations  # PR create/merge/view
    signals: SignalStore           # signal file read/write

docs.title: 5. OCP: Adding a new AgentRole requires modifying existing code
docs.text:
The prompt dispatch in run_graph.py is an if/elif chain that must be extended for every new role. Similarly, validate_task_paths() has role-specific if/elif chains.

Recommendation: Registry dict mapping AgentRole to a prompt builder callable. New roles can be registered without modifying existing dispatch code:

PROMPT_BUILDERS: dict[AgentRole, PromptBuilder] = {
    AgentRole.SPEC_WRITER: build_spec_writer_prompt,
    AgentRole.TEST_WRITER: build_test_writer_prompt,
    # ...
}

docs.title: 6. ISP: TaskState couples agent-backend-specific state into the core model
docs.text:
agent_task.py TaskState:

@dataclass
class TaskState:
    status: TaskStatus = TaskStatus.PENDING
    worktree_path: Path | None = None
    branch_name: str | None = None
    tmux_session: str | None = None    # tmux-specific
    pane_id: str | None = None          # tmux-specific
    pr_url: str | None = None
    ...

tmux_session and pane_id are tmux-specific details that won't apply to other harnesses. These should move behind the AgentHarness abstraction — the harness tracks its own handles internally, and TaskState retains only orchestrator-level concerns (status, PR URL, retries).

docs.title: Prioritized Roadmap (SOLID only)
docs.text: Priority Change SOLID principle Why this order 1 Extract prompt_builder.py from run_graph.py SRP Pure move, no behavior change; makes run_graph.py readable 2 Split task_launcher.py into focused modules SRP, ISP Prerequisite for introducing protocols 3 Define AgentHarness protocol + extract ClaudeCodeTmuxHarness DIP, OCP The pluggability enabler 4 Remove tmux-specific fields from TaskState ISP Clean separation once harness exists 5 Introduce VCSOperations / CodeHostOperations protocols DIP Enables non-GitHub hosting 6 Registry-based prompt dispatch + path validation OCP Nice-to-have extensibility

docs.title: Part 2: Class Encapsulation Opportunities
docs.text: Reviewing the codebase through the lens of repeated parameter groups and feature envy reveals five clear places where free functions should become classes.

docs.title: A. (graph_name, target_repo_root) — passed together 16 times
docs.text:
This is the biggest offender. Functions across task_launcher.py and reset_graph.py — pull_graph_branch, create_final_pr, record_run_start, read_run_info, list_remote_task_branches, delete_local_graph_branch, graph_branch_exists_on_remote, merge_history_path, record_gate_failure, scan_adr_section, write_adr_index_to_graph_branch, _close_open_prs, _remove_workflow_dir — all take the same pair.

Missing class: Something like GraphRepo — initialized with graph_name and target_repo_root, with all these operations as methods. This is distinct from AgentTaskGraph (the in-memory task DAG); GraphRepo owns the on-disk git/GitHub operations for a graph run.

docs.title: B. (task, graph_branch, graph) — the prompt builder trio, 6 functions
docs.text:
Every _build_*_prompt in run_graph.py takes (task: AgentTask, graph_branch: str, graph: AgentTaskGraph | None = None) and then calls the same helpers (_adr_step, _spec_reading_step, _resolve_gate, _effective_verbosity) which read the same fields from task and graph.

Missing class: PromptBuilder — initialized with task, graph_branch, and graph. Shared helpers become methods. The role-specific builders dispatch via a dict registry. This also naturally hosts the role→builder registry from the OCP finding above.

docs.title: C. signal_dir: Path — constructed, then passed 10+ times
docs.text:
The signal dir path target_repo_root / ".workflow" / graph_name / "signals" / task_id is computed in the orchestrator and then threaded through to write_context, write_instructions, save_agent_log, poll_for_completion_at, read_done_note_at, read_design_concerns, save_pr_summary, etc.

Note: AgentTaskGraph.signal_dir() is already the single source of truth for computing the path — the issue is that the operations on that directory are scattered free functions.

Missing class: SignalDirectory — wraps a Path and provides the read/write methods. Thin, but it eliminates the repeated signal_dir parameter threading and centralizes file conventions (.done, .failed, .merged, context.md, instructions.md).

docs.title: D. task: AgentTask passed just to read task.state.pane_id — 4 functions
docs.text:
• save_agent_log(task, signal_dir) — reads only task.state.pane_id
  • close_agent_pane(task) — reads only task.state.pane_id
  • remove_worktree(task, target_repo_root) — reads only task.state.worktree_path and task.state.branch_name
  • neutralize_pixi_lock_in_pr(task) — reads only task.state.worktree_path and task.state.branch_name

Classic feature envy — these functions want TaskState fields, not the full AgentTask. This resolves naturally when tmux/pane operations move into AgentHarness (priority 3 above) and worktree operations become methods on a worktree manager. The agent handle replaces passing the whole task object.

docs.title: E. pr_url: str — threaded through 5+ functions
docs.text: merge_pr, pixi_toml_changed_in_pr, append_concerns_to_pr, save_pr_summary, and the merger prompt builder all take a PR URL and shell out to gh pr .... A PRHandle class (initialized with the URL, and optionally the parsed owner/repo/number) would consolidate these and eliminate the URL-parsing duplication.

docs.title: Updated Prioritized Roadmap (SOLID + class encapsulation)
docs.text:
Priority Change Addresses 1 Extract PromptBuilder class from run_graph.py SRP + Finding B 2 Split task_launcher.py into modules, extracting GraphRepo (A), SignalDirectory (C), PRHandle (E) SRP + ISP + Findings A/C/E 3 Define AgentHarness protocol + extract ClaudeCodeTmuxHarness DIP + OCP + resolves Finding D 4 Remove tmux-specific fields from TaskState ISP 5 VCSOperations / CodeHostOperations protocols DIP 6 Registry-based prompt dispatch OCP

Steps 1–2 are pure refactors with no behavior change. The class extractions make step 3 (the pluggability enabler) much cleaner because the harness protocol boundary is obvious — it wraps the tmux functions that were already grouped together.

docs.title: Architectural Review: SOLID Analysis & Cleanup Opportunities
1. SRP — run_graph.py does too much
docs.text:
Problem: The orchestrator (async loop, task dispatch, signal handling) is mixed with ~800 lines of prompt generation templates. These are completely different concerns.

Fix: Extract prompt builders into a dedicated prompt_builders/ package (one module per role, or a strategy pattern). run_graph.py should only orchestrate — not know the content of agent instructions.

Files affected: src/agentrelay/run_graph.py

docs.title: 2. OCP — New roles require modifying core code
docs.text:
Problem: Adding a new AgentRole requires touching _build_task_instructions(), validate_task_paths(), and potentially from_yaml(). These are scattered if/elif chains.

Fix: A role registry / strategy pattern: 

class PromptStrategy(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def build_prompt(self, task, graph) -> str: ...
    @abstractmethod
    def validate_paths(self, task) -> list[str]: ...  # returns errors

PROMPT_STRATEGIES: dict[AgentRole, PromptStrategy] = {
    AgentRole.SPEC_WRITER: SpecWriterStrategy(),
    ...
}

docs.title: 3. DIP — High-level code depends on low-level subprocess calls (critical for pluggability)
docs.text:
Problem: The two biggest blocks of Claude-specific, tmux-specific code:

a) Agent executor (task_launcher.py — tmux + claude CLI): 

# Currently hardcoded:
["tmux", "new-window", ...]
['claude', '--dangerously-skip-permissions', '--add-dir', ...]

b) VCS/GitHub backend (task_launcher.py — git + gh): 

# Currently hardcoded:
["git", "worktree", "add", ...]
["gh", "pr", "merge", pr_url, "--merge"]

Fix: Two abstractions:

class AgentExecutor(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def launch(self, cwd, task_id, signal_dir, model) -> str: ...
    @abstractmethod
    def send_prompt(self, executor_id, prompt) -> None: ...
    @abstractmethod
    def capture_output(self, executor_id) -> str: ...
    @abstractmethod
    def close(self, executor_id) -> None: ...

class TmuxClaudeExecutor(AgentExecutor): ...   # current behavior
class HeadlessExecutor(AgentExecutor): ...      # for tests
class CopilotExecutor(AgentExecutor): ...       # future

class VCSBackend(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def create_worktree(self, path, branch, base) -> None: ...
    @abstractmethod
    def merge_pr(self, pr_url) -> None: ...
    @abstractmethod
    def create_pr(self, title, body, base, head) -> str: ...

class GitHubBackend(VCSBackend): ...   # current
class GitLabBackend(VCSBackend): ...   # future

docs.title: 4. ISP — task_launcher.py is a monolith (1,106 lines)
docs.text:
Problem: A single module mixes git operations, tmux operations, signal file I/O, and PR management. Every caller imports from this kitchen-sink.

Fix: Split into focused modules: - vcs.py — git/worktree/branch operations - pr_manager.py — PR create/merge/view/close - agent_executor.py — tmux/Claude launch/prompt/log - signal_io.py — read/write .done, .failed, .merged, task_context.json - adr.py — ADR scanning and index writing

docs.title: 5. Frozen/mutable contract in AgentTask
docs.text:
Problem: @dataclass(frozen=True) but state: TaskState = field(default_factory=TaskState) is mutable. This is intentional but violates the stated contract and confuses readers.

Fix: Either drop frozen=True (since it isn't enforced in spirit) or separate AgentTask (identity, truly frozen) from a separate TaskRun object that holds mutable TaskState. The graph would hold dict[str, TaskRun] instead of dict[str, AgentTask].

docs.title: Prioritized Roadmap
docs.text:
Priority Change Scope Why 1 Extract prompt builders run_graph.py → prompt_builders/ Unblocks new roles w/o touching orchestrator 2 AgentExecutor abstraction task_launcher.py Direct path to Copilot/Codex pluggability 3 Split task_launcher.py 1 file → 4–5 files Makes VCS backend extraction tractable 4 VCSBackend abstraction after #3 GitLab/Gitea support 5 Role strategy registry run_graph.py + agent_task.py Clean OCP fix for new roles 6 Fix AgentTask frozen/mutable agent_task.py Low risk, improves clarity

Items 1–3 are the highest leverage — they reduce run_graph.py from ~950 lines to ~300, make task_launcher.py testable in isolation, and give you a clean seam for the pluggable harness feature in the backlog.

docs.title: Architecture Review — 2026-03-10 (Opus 4.6)
docs.text: High-level review of the non-prototype architecture prior to implementing concrete adapter PRs (I–Q from sprint 2026-03-09).

docs.title: 1. Immutable spec / mutable runtime split
docs.text: The strongest design decision in the codebase. Task, TaskGraph, WorkstreamSpec are all frozen; TaskRuntime, WorkstreamRuntime are mutable envelopes. This makes the data flow easy to reason about — specs flow down, state mutates in place, and the boundary between "what to do" and "what's happening" is always clear.

docs.title: 2. The TaskRunner state machine is clean
docs.text: The lifecycle in task_runner.py is linear and explicit: PENDING → RUNNING → PR_CREATED → PR_MERGED, with FAILED as a sink. The ALLOWED_TASK_TRANSITIONS mapping makes legal edges auditable. The try/except structure around each IO call is repetitive but correct — every failure path records the error and transitions to FAILED deterministically.

docs.title: 3. TaskGraph validates eagerly at construction
docs.text: No invalid graph can exist. Cycle detection, workstream hierarchy checks, dependency existence, key-ID consistency — all enforced in __init__. The MappingProxyType wrapping prevents external mutation. This is the right approach for a DAG that gets built once and queried many times.

docs.title: 4. Clean protocol boundaries
docs.text: TaskRunnerIO and the integration_contracts protocols are well-factored. The four adapter protocols (workspace, signal, PR, agent launcher) map to real operational boundaries. The IntegrationAdapters bundle is a natural composition point.

docs.title: 1. Two parallel protocol layers for the same boundary
docs.text:
TaskRunnerIO (in task_runner.py:69) and the four integration_contracts protocols (WorkspaceAdapter, SignalAdapter, PullRequestAdapter, AgentLauncher) describe overlapping concerns. The sprint plan says PR N will compose adapters into a concrete TaskRunnerIO, but the shape mismatch is notable:

  • TaskRunnerIO.prepare() takes only TaskRuntime; WorkspaceAdapter.ensure_workspace() takes both TaskRuntime and WorkstreamRuntime.
  • TaskRunnerIO has no concept of WorkstreamRuntime at all — it's a single-task interface. But workspace provisioning inherently needs workstream context (branch strategy, shared worktree).
  • integration_contracts.CompletionSignal has a concerns field; task_runner.TaskCompletionSignal does not.

This will require either an adapter/translation layer that papers over the differences, or a refactor to align the two. Worth deciding now before concrete implementations exist.

docs.title: 2. Task.dependencies stores Task objects, not IDs
docs.text: task.py:180 — dependencies: tuple["Task", ...]. This means every Task carries references to its full dependency tree. The builder must construct tasks in topological order and thread object references through. TaskGraph then also stores _dependency_ids as a separate index built by walking task.dependencies and extracting IDs. This creates two representations of the same information. The YAML builder already works in ID space and converts to objects at the end. If Task.dependencies were tuple[str, ...] (just IDs), the builder and graph construction would simplify. The tradeoff is that task.dependencies gives you direct object access without a graph lookup — but in practice, every caller that needs dependency info already has a TaskGraph handle.

docs.title: 3. Agent on TaskRuntime is an odd fit
docs.text: task_runtime/runtime.py:113 — agent: Optional[Agent] = None. The runtime envelope bundles immutable spec + mutable state + artifacts + a live process handle. The agent reference is set by TaskRunner after launch (task_runner.py:219), but it's only used within that same run() call scope. Nothing outside TaskRunner.run() reads runtime.agent. Storing a live process handle on a data container that gets passed around and returned in OrchestratorResult is a latent lifecycle hazard — if someone holds a reference to the result after teardown, runtime.agent points to a dead process.

docs.title: 4. The orchestrator does a lot of direct state mutation
docs.text: orchestrator.py directly mutates TaskRuntime.state.status, TaskRuntime.state.error, WorkstreamRuntime.state.* in multiple places — the run() method, _mark_blocked_pending_tasks_failed(), _mark_inflight_tasks_failed(), _refresh_workstream_terminal_states(). The TaskRunner also mutates the same runtime. This dual-writer pattern is manageable now because the orchestrator only touches runtimes that aren't currently in a TaskRunner.run() call, but that invariant isn't enforced — it's a convention. Something like retry (resetting FAILED → PENDING at orchestrator.py:318) directly overwrites status that the TaskRunner just set.

docs.title: 5. integration_errors is elaborate but not yet consumed
docs.text: The error hierarchy (7 exception classes + classifier function) is well- structured, but nothing in task_runner.py or orchestrator.py catches IntegrationError or calls classify_integration_error(). The TaskRunner catches bare Exception at every IO boundary and does f"{type(exc).__name__}: {exc}" — no classification, no boundary tagging. The integration errors are designed to be thrown by concrete adapters and caught by... something. But the current TaskRunner doesn't distinguish expected-task-failure from internal-error at the IO level. This gap needs to be resolved when wiring concrete adapters — either the TaskRunner needs to use classify_integration_error(), or the distinction only matters at the orchestrator level (which already has TaskOutcomeClass).

docs.title: 6. RemoteWorkspaceRef is speculative
docs.text: integration_contracts/__init__.py:35-54 has 6 optional fields for a remote execution model that doesn't exist yet and may never look like this. workspace_uri, repo_ref, execution_target, artifacts_uri are placeholder guesses. This is the one place the codebase speculates about future needs rather than designing for today's requirements.

docs.title: 7. Workstream hierarchy depth — is it needed?
docs.text: max_workstream_depth defaults to 1 (parent→child only). The validation machinery for acyclic hierarchies, depth limits, and ancestor-chain walking in the orchestrator is thorough. But the sprint plan and existing YAML examples only show flat workstreams. The parent/child hierarchy adds ~100 lines of validation + orchestrator logic for a feature with no concrete use case yet. Not necessarily wrong — it may reflect a real need — but worth flagging.

docs.title: 8. The _transition_to_failed escape hatch
docs.text: task_runner.py:279-286 has a fallback that force-sets status = FAILED even when FAILED isn't in ALLOWED_TASK_TRANSITIONS[current_status]. This bypasses the transition table for cases where the current status is a terminal that shouldn't reach FAILED (like PR_MERGED). It's defensive, but it means the transition table isn't actually authoritative — there's a backdoor. In practice this path can't be hit from _record_io_failure() because IO failures only happen during RUNNING, but the code doesn't make that guarantee visible.

docs.title: 9. No logging or observability hooks
docs.text: OrchestratorEvent is a good structured-event model, but events are only accumulated in a list and returned at the end. There's no callback/listener/logging hook for real-time observability during a run. For debugging a multi-agent orchestration, you'd want to see events as they happen, not just in a post-mortem result. This is likely fine for now but will matter immediately when running real workloads.

docs.title: Summary of risk areas for the adapter PRs ahead
docs.text: The biggest design tension is item 1 — how TaskRunnerIO (single-task, no workstream awareness) composes with integration_contracts (workstream-aware adapters). This needs a clear resolution before PR N. The other items are lower risk — things to clean up opportunistically as you go.

docs.title: Review Findings (non-prototype track, highest impact first)
docs.text:
1. The current architecture layer intentionally stops before orchestration, so the core gap is real runtime execution. docs/WORKFLOW.md:12, docs/WORKFLOW.md:16, docs/BACKLOG.md:9

  2. Agent launch/kickoff in the non-prototype layer is still stubbed, so an orchestrator cannot yet dispatch real work there. src/agentrelay/agent/agent.py:83, src/agentrelay/agent/agent.py:116

  3. There is no non-prototype TaskGraph/YAML loader yet; only v01 has that (AgentTaskGraphBuilder). src/agentrelay/prototypes/v01/agent_task_graph.py:103

  4. v01 proves the loop, but it is heavily coupled/monolithic; avoid porting it 1:1 into core. src/agentrelay/prototypes/v01/run_graph.py:697, src/agentrelay/prototypes/v01/task_launcher.py:135

pixi run test currently passes (500 passed), so baseline is clean.

docs.title: Your specific questions
docs.text:
• TaskGraph next? Yes. Implement it now.

  • YAML -> runtime: TaskGraphBuilder or just TaskBuilder? Use both layers:

  • TaskGraphBuilder: YAML -> validated TaskGraph (IDs, deps, cycle checks, defaults).

  • TaskRuntimeBuilder (or factory): TaskGraph -> dict[str, TaskRuntime].

  • Should Task run itself? No. Keep Task immutable spec-only (task.py). Use TaskRunner for execution.

  • Implement Orchestrator? Yes, but as a thin scheduler over TaskRuntime; delegate per-task lifecycle to TaskRunner.

docs.title: Original recommended implementation order (non-prototype, reference)
docs.text:
1. task_graph.py: TaskGraph + validation/query APIs (ready, dependents, topo/cycle checks).
  2. task_graph/builder.py: YAML schema + TaskGraphBuilder.from_yaml(...).
  3. task_runtime/builder.py: initialize TaskRuntime objects from graph.
  4. task_runner.py: one-task lifecycle state machine (PENDING -> RUNNING -> PR_CREATED -> PR_MERGED/FAILED).
  5. orchestrator.py: async dispatch loop + dependency scheduling + resume hooks.
  6. Adapter layer for side effects (git/worktree, signals, PR, agent launch), then wire TmuxAgent real implementation.

docs.title: Revised implementation plan (PR-based)
docs.text:
1. PR A: lock TaskRunner API contract (interface-only) and docs/diagram wiring. Status: merged on March 9, 2026 (task_runner.py exists, run(...) still stubbed).
  2. PR B: add workstream domain model + task reference (workstream_id with compatibility defaults).
  3. PR C: extend TaskGraph to include workstreams + validation/query APIs.
  4. PR D: extend YAML schema and TaskGraphBuilder for top-level workstreams + task mapping.
  5. PR E: add workstream runtime model + builder initialization from graph.
  6. PR F: implement TaskRunner lifecycle behavior against TaskRunnerIO.
  7. PR G: implement orchestrator.py scheduling loop with dependency + workstream rules. Note: in PR G, classify TaskRunner outcomes so orchestrator retry/logging behavior is explicit:
  8. TaskRunner.run(...) returns with FAILED status => expected task-level failure (retry policy may apply).
  9. TaskRunner.run(...) raises => internal/system failure (log traceback, fail-fast, no automatic retry).
  10. PR H: docs/examples/migration pass for new schema + orchestrator behavior.

docs.title: Mapping revised plan to original items
docs.text:
1. Original item 4 (task_runner.py) is split across PR A (contract) and PR F (behavior).
  2. Original item 5 (orchestrator.py) is primarily PR G, with dependencies delivered in PRs B-F.
  3. Original item 6 (side-effect adapters + real TmuxAgent) is partially prepared by PRs F-G and then completed in follow-on adapter/agent implementation work.

docs.title: Clarification on completion of original item 5
docs.text:
1. Yes: once PRs A-H are complete, we should be effectively done with the original item 5 scope (orchestrator.py async dispatch + dependency scheduling + resume hooks).
  2. Note: full production side-effect integration (original item 6) remains a distinct follow-up track.

docs.title: Sprint Notes — 2026-03-09
docs.text: Status: Cancelled. Replaced by docs/sprints/2026-03-10-arch-improvements.md and docs/sprints/2026-03-12.md. Keeping this document for reference.

docs.title: Phase 2 plan: original item 6 (adapter layer + real runner)
External testbed assumptions
docs.text:
1. External repo for manual integration/E2E work: /data/git/agentrelaydemos/main/.
  2. New-style YAML graphs for non-prototype runner tests will live under /data/git/agentrelaydemos/main/graphs/.
  3. Old prototype graph files remain archived under /data/git/agentrelaydemos/main/graphs/archived/.
  4. During early PRs, it is acceptable to use synthetic temp repos for deterministic tests and use agentrelaydemos for manual smoke checks.

docs.title: PR I: integration contracts + error model
docs.text:
• Branch: feat/integration-contracts
  • Scope:
  • Define non-prototype adapter interfaces for workspace, signal I/O, PR ops, and agent launch boundaries.
  • Define typed exceptions and error classification mapping (expected task failure vs internal/system failure).
  • Document invariants in architecture/diagram.
  • Acceptance-test checklist:
  • [x] New contracts compile cleanly (pixi run typecheck).
  • [x] Unit tests cover classification and exception mapping behavior.
  • [x] No behavioral changes to existing task/orchestrator tests.

docs.title: PR I implementation plan (detailed)
docs.text:
1. Create a new integration package (contracts only)
  2. Add:
    • src/agentrelay/integration_contracts/__init__.py
    • src/agentrelay/integration_errors/__init__.py

  3. Purpose: define stable side-effect boundaries without changing runtime behavior.

  4. Define adapter contracts (Protocol interfaces)

  5. In integration_contracts, define protocols for four boundaries:
    • WorkspaceAdapter (generalized from local worktree-specific naming)
    • SignalAdapter
    • PullRequestAdapter
    • AgentLauncher
  6. Define generalized workspace refs for local/remote execution:
    • LocalWorkspaceRef
    • RemoteWorkspaceRef
    • WorkspaceRef (union of local/remote refs)

  7. Add IntegrationAdapters dataclass to bundle boundaries for later TaskRunnerIO composition (PR N).

  8. Define typed error model + classification

  9. In integration_errors, define:
    • IntegrationError (base)
    • Boundary-specific subclasses (WorkspaceIntegrationError, SignalIntegrationError, PullRequestIntegrationError, AgentLaunchIntegrationError)
    • Compatibility alias WorktreeIntegrationError (transitional)
    • ExpectedTaskFailureError vs InternalIntegrationError
    • classify_integration_error(exc) returning IntegrationFailureClass

  10. Goal: make expected task failure vs internal/system failure explicit before concrete adapters.

  11. Keep behavior unchanged in existing runtime code

  12. No real wiring into TaskRunner/Orchestrator in PR I.

  13. This PR remains contract-and-model only so downstream adapter PRs can implement against a stable API.

  14. Docs + diagram updates

  15. Update:

    • docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (integration boundary layer)
    • docs/DIAGRAM.md (integration package/module block)
    • API docs/nav entries for integration package as needed.

  16. Tests + validation

  17. Add unit tests:
    • test/test_integration_errors.py
    • test/test_integration_contracts.py
  18. Cover:
    • error class hierarchy
    • classification helper behavior
    • immutability/shape of contract dataclasses
  19. Run:
    • pixi run pytest test/test_integration_errors.py test/test_integration_contracts.py
    • pixi run typecheck
    • pixi run docs-build

docs.title: Sprint Notes — 2026-03-10: Architecture Improvements
docs.text: Paused the phase 2 adapter work (sprint 2026-03-09) to address findings from an architecture review of the non-prototype codebase. The review was conducted by Opus 4.6 and documented in docs/reviews/2026-03-10-Opus-46.md.

docs.title: Strengths identified (no action needed)
docs.text:
1. Immutable spec / mutable runtime split — Task, TaskGraph, WorkstreamSpec are frozen; TaskRuntime, WorkstreamRuntime are mutable envelopes. Clean data flow.

  2. TaskRunner state machine — linear lifecycle with explicit transition table. Repetitive try/except around each IO call is correct.

  3. TaskGraph eager validation — no invalid graph can exist. Cycle detection, hierarchy checks, dependency existence all enforced at construction.

  4. Clean protocol boundaries — TaskRunnerIO and adapter protocols map to real operational boundaries.

docs.title: Concerns addressed
docs.text: # Concern Resolution PR 1 Two parallel protocol layers (TaskRunnerIO vs integration_contracts) Decomposed TaskRunnerIO into per-step protocols (TaskPreparer, TaskLauncher, etc.), added WorkstreamRunner/WorkstreamRunnerIO, deleted integration_contracts/ #78 2 Task.dependencies stores Task objects, not IDs Changed to tuple[str, ...], removed redundant validation, simplified builder #79 3 Agent on TaskRuntime is an odd fit Removed live Agent field, added agent_address: AgentAddress to TaskArtifacts as audit trail #80 4 Orchestrator does a lot of direct state mutation Added read-only view protocols (part A), then added mutation methods to runtimes and replaced ~15 direct field assignments in orchestrator (part B) #81, #82 5 integration_errors elaborate but not consumed Renamed to errors/, wired classify_integration_error() into TaskRunner._record_io_failure(), added failure_class to TaskRunResult, orchestrator now distinguishes internal vs expected failures #83 6 RemoteWorkspaceRef is speculative Removed RemoteWorkspaceRef and kind discriminator from LocalWorkspaceRef #84 7 Workstream hierarchy depth — is it needed? Kept as-is. The hierarchy validation and orchestrator ancestor-chain logic will be used when we create nested workstreams. No code change. — 8 _transition_to_failed escape hatch Removed silent fallback that bypassed transition table; method now delegates to _transition() unconditionally after idempotency check #85 9 No logging or observability hooks Added OrchestratorListener protocol with on_event() callback; orchestrator accepts optional listener, notifies at all 6 event emission sites #86

docs.title: Sprint Notes — 2026-03-12
docs.text: Status: Superseded by 2026-03-13. PR J shipped; remaining PRs revised after defining the agent communication protocol (docs/AGENT_COMM_PROTOCOL.md).

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Implement concrete classes that satisfy the protocol boundaries already defined in task_runner/io.py and workstream/io.py, complete the TmuxAgent stubs, and provide a CLI entry point — making the current architecture runnable end-to-end.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The current architecture defines a complete scheduling and lifecycle layer:

  • Orchestrator schedules tasks via dependency + workstream constraints, delegating one-task execution to anything satisfying TaskRunnerLike.
  • TaskRunner drives the PENDING → RUNNING → PR_CREATED → PR_MERGED state machine, delegating each lifecycle step to the six per-step protocols composed in TaskRunnerIO.
  • WorkstreamRunner drives workstream prepare/merge/teardown, delegating to the three per-step protocols in WorkstreamRunnerIO.

All of this is tested (~624 tests) against in-memory fakes. What's missing are concrete implementations of the nine per-step protocols and a CLI that wires everything together.

docs.title: Protocol → concrete operation mapping
docs.text:
Each protocol maps to a small set of subprocess operations already proven in the v01 prototype (prototypes/v01/task_launcher.py, run_graph.py):

Protocol What the implementation does Infrastructure needed TaskPreparer Create git worktree + branch, create signal dir, write task_context.json and instructions.md git worktree ops, signal file I/O, prompt/instruction builders TaskLauncher Create tmux window, launch Claude Code in it, return TmuxAgent tmux ops, TmuxAgent.from_config() TaskKickoff Wait for Claude TUI readiness, send kickoff prompt via tmux tmux ops, TmuxAgent.send_kickoff() TaskCompletionChecker Poll signal dir for .done / .failed, parse into TaskCompletionSignal signal file I/O TaskMerger gh pr merge, pull main / graph branch to sync local refs gh CLI ops, git fetch/pull TaskTeardown Capture agent log, close tmux pane, remove worktree + branch tmux ops, git worktree ops WorkstreamPreparer Create graph/<name> integration branch, push to origin git branch ops WorkstreamMerger Create PR from graph branch → main, merge it gh CLI ops WorkstreamTeardown Delete graph branch (local + remote) git branch ops

docs.title: What this means for PR structure
docs.text:
The previous sprint plan (2026-03-09) organized PRs around "adapters" — one per infrastructure domain (git adapter, signal adapter, tmux adapter, PR adapter). That framing was vague about how the adapters plugged into the existing protocols. This plan instead organizes work around the protocol implementations themselves, with infrastructure modules as supporting internals.

The natural layers are:

  1. Infrastructure primitives — thin subprocess wrappers for git, tmux, and gh operations. No agentrelay domain types — just Path, str, and subprocess calls. These are internal implementation details, not public API.

  2. Protocol implementations — classes that satisfy TaskPreparer, TaskLauncher, etc. by composing infrastructure primitives and operating on TaskRuntime / WorkstreamRuntime. This is where the design connects.

  3. Instruction builders — per-role text generators that produce the instructions.md content the TaskPreparer writes to the signal directory.

  4. Composition + CLI — a factory that assembles TaskRunnerIO / WorkstreamRunnerIO from the concrete implementations, and a CLI entry point that wires Orchestrator + TaskRunner + TaskGraphBuilder.

docs.title: PR J: Infrastructure primitives
docs.text:
• Branch: feat/infra-primitives
  • New package: src/agentrelay/infra/

Thin, stateless subprocess wrappers. No domain types — just paths and strings. These are the building blocks that protocol implementations will compose.

Modules:

Module Functions Used by protocols git.py worktree_add(), worktree_remove(), branch_create(), branch_delete(), pull_ff_only(), fetch_branch(), update_local_ref(), push_branch(), ls_remote_branch_exists() TaskPreparer, TaskTeardown, TaskMerger, WorkstreamPreparer, WorkstreamMerger, WorkstreamTeardown tmux.py new_window(), send_keys(), capture_pane(), kill_window(), wait_for_tui_ready() TaskLauncher, TaskKickoff, TaskTeardown gh.py pr_merge(), pr_create(), pr_body() TaskMerger, WorkstreamMerger signals.py ensure_signal_dir(), write_json(), write_text(), read_signal_file(), poll_signal_files() TaskPreparer, TaskCompletionChecker, TaskTeardown

Design notes: - Functions in infra/ are pure wrappers: they take paths/strings and call subprocess. They raise subprocess.CalledProcessError on failure — the protocol implementations handle error mapping. - infra/ is a private implementation detail (prefixed with _ in imports or excluded from __init__.py re-exports). It is not part of the public API and does not appear in the design diagram as a first-class package. - The <<module>> stereotype convention from CLAUDE.md applies here.

Acceptance criteria: - [ ] Each function tested against temp git repo fixtures (real git, not mocked). - [ ] tmux functions tested via subprocess mock (no live tmux session required). - [ ] gh functions tested via subprocess mock. - [ ] Signal functions tested against temp directories. - [ ] pixi run check passes.

docs.text:
• Branch: feat/instruction-builders
  • New module: src/agentrelay/instructions.py (or package if needed)

Per-role instruction text generators. The TaskPreparer will call these to produce the instructions.md content written to the signal directory. Each builder takes a Task (and optionally TaskRuntime for attempt-aware instructions) and returns a string.

Scope: - Builder functions for each AgentRole: generic, spec_writer, test_writer, test_reviewer, implementer. - Incorporate completion gate instructions, review-on-attempt logic, and signal file write instructions (how the agent should write .done/.failed). - Reference prototype: prototypes/v01/run_graph.py prompt construction.

Acceptance criteria: - [ ] Each role produces well-formed instruction text. - [ ] Instructions include signal file protocol (write .done with PR URL on line 2, or .failed with reason on line 2). - [ ] Completion gate instructions included when task.completion_gate is set. - [ ] pixi run check passes.

docs.title: PR L: Task-level protocol implementations
docs.text:
• Branch: feat/task-protocol-impls
  • New package: src/agentrelay/execution/ (or similar)

Concrete classes implementing all six TaskRunnerIO protocols. Each class composes infrastructure primitives from infra/ and instruction builders from PR K.

Classes and the protocols they satisfy:

Class Satisfies Key operations WorktreeTaskPreparer TaskPreparer git.worktree_add(), signals.ensure_signal_dir(), signals.write_json() (task_context), signals.write_text() (instructions.md via builder) TmuxTaskLauncher TaskLauncher tmux.new_window(), launches Claude Code, returns TmuxAgent TmuxTaskKickoff TaskKickoff Delegates to agent.send_kickoff() (which uses tmux.wait_for_tui_ready() + tmux.send_keys()) SignalCompletionChecker TaskCompletionChecker signals.poll_signal_files(), parses .done/.failed into TaskCompletionSignal GhTaskMerger TaskMerger gh.pr_merge(), git.pull_ff_only() or git.fetch_branch() + git.update_local_ref() WorktreeTaskTeardown TaskTeardown tmux.capture_pane() (agent log), tmux.kill_window(), git.worktree_remove(), git.branch_delete()

Also in this PR: complete the TmuxAgent stubs.

TmuxAgent.from_config() and TmuxAgent.send_kickoff() are currently NotImplementedError stubs. This PR implements them using infra/tmux.py functions. The TaskLauncher and TaskKickoff protocol implementations delegate to TmuxAgent methods.

Acceptance criteria: - [ ] Each class satisfies its protocol (verified by isinstance checks with runtime_checkable protocols). - [ ] Unit tests with mocked infra/ functions cover success and failure paths. - [ ] Error mapping: subprocess.CalledProcessError from infra functions is wrapped in appropriate IntegrationError subclasses. - [ ] TaskRunnerIO can be constructed from all six implementations. - [ ] pixi run check passes.

The current Orchestrator manages workstream prepare/merge/teardown state directly via inline logic. This PR refactors the orchestrator to delegate workstream lifecycle operations to WorkstreamRunner, mirroring how it already delegates task lifecycle to TaskRunnerLike.

Scope: 1. Orchestrator accepts a WorkstreamRunner (or a factory/protocol for one) in addition to TaskRunnerLike. 2. Workstream prepare, merge, and teardown calls go through WorkstreamRunner instead of being handled inline. 3. Existing orchestrator tests updated to supply a WorkstreamRunner (can use in-memory fakes, same pattern as TaskRunnerLike tests).

Acceptance criteria: - [ ] Orchestrator delegates workstream lifecycle to WorkstreamRunner. - [ ] All existing orchestrator tests pass (updated to provide WorkstreamRunner). - [ ] No change to external Orchestrator behavior — purely an internal refactor. - [ ] pixi run check passes.

docs.title: Sprint Notes — 2026-03-13
docs.text: Status: Planning.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Same end goal as the 2026-03-12 sprint — make the current architecture runnable end-to-end — but with PR K and onward redesigned around the agent communication protocol defined in docs/AGENT_COMM_PROTOCOL.md.

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
The 2026-03-12 sprint shipped PR J (ops/ infrastructure primitives) and began work on PR K (instruction builders). During PR K development, we identified that the instruction builders were porting the prototype's monolithic approach — mixing role-specific work with git commands, signaling logic, and framework-specific actions in a single text blob.

We paused to design a clean communication protocol from first principles (PR #91, docs/AGENT_COMM_PROTOCOL.md). The protocol defines five layers:

  1. Task manifest (manifest.json) — structured facts about the task
  2. Work instructions (instructions.md) — role-specific work only, template-driven for formulaic roles
  3. Workflow policies (policies.json) — composable JSON, abstract vocabulary that adapters translate
  4. Signaling contract — abstract signal definitions (done, failed, concern, gate_attempt)
  5. Framework adapter — maps onto existing TaskRunnerIO protocol implementations

This sprint picks up from where 2026-03-12 left off, with the protocol as the foundation for all remaining work.

docs.title: Completed prerequisites
docs.text: PR What Branch/PR PR J ops/ infrastructure primitives package PR #88, merged Protocol docs/AGENT_COMM_PROTOCOL.md PR #91, merged

docs.title: What the protocol changes
docs.text:
• PR K is no longer "port prototype instruction builders." It's now "build the framework-agnostic protocol layers (manifest, policies, templates, spec representation)."
  • PR L's TaskPreparer now writes manifest.json + policies.json + resolved instructions.md (via template or custom), not monolithic instruction text.
  • PRs M–P are mostly unchanged.

docs.title: PR K: Protocol schemas, builders, and templates
docs.text:
• Branch: feat/protocol-schemas

Framework-agnostic data types and builders for protocol layers 1-3. No shell commands, no framework-specific data anywhere in this PR.

Modules:

Module Responsibility protocol/manifest.py TaskManifest dataclass + build_manifest(task, runtime) → dict protocol/policies.py WorkflowPolicies dataclass + build_policies(task, runtime) → dict protocol/templates.py resolve_instructions(role, manifest, adapter_name?) → str — loads and parameterizes role templates spec/ package SpecRepresentation protocol + PythonStubSpec first impl

Templates directory: src/agentrelay/templates/

Shared role templates as markdown files:

File Role spec_writer.md Creative spec writing — parameterized by spec representation type test_writer.md Write tests against specs — parameterized by paths test_reviewer.md Review tests — parameterized by paths implementer.md Implement code, pass tests — parameterized by paths + gate

Adapter-specific overrides go in templates/<adapter_name>/ (e.g., templates/claude_code/test_writer.md). Resolution order: adapter-specific > shared > per-task custom.

Design notes: - Manifest builder extracts data from Task + TaskRuntime and produces the JSON structure defined in AGENT_COMM_PROTOCOL.md Layer 1. - Policies builder reads Task.completion_gate, Task.review, etc. and produces the composable JSON structure from Layer 3. - Template resolver uses Task.role to select template, substitutes {src_paths}, {test_paths}, etc. from manifest data. Falls back to per-task instructions.md for generic role. - SpecRepresentation protocol defines how spec formats are described to agents. PythonStubSpec = Python files with docstrings and raise NotImplementedError.

Acceptance criteria: - [ ] Manifest builder produces valid JSON matching the protocol schema. - [ ] Policies builder produces valid JSON matching the protocol schema. - [ ] Template resolver loads templates, substitutes parameters correctly. - [ ] generic role falls back to custom instructions. - [ ] SpecRepresentation protocol and PythonStubSpec are defined. - [ ] pixi run check passes.

Also in this PR: Complete TmuxAgent stubs (from_config() and send_kickoff()) using ops/tmux.py.

docs.title: Resolved questions
docs.text:
Carried forward from 2026-03-12 where still applicable:

  1. Package name for protocol implementations. Resolved in PR #88: each package uses core/ + implementations/ subpackages.

  2. Instruction builder scope. Superseded: PR K now builds protocol layers 1-3 per AGENT_COMM_PROTOCOL.md. Role-specific instructions use templates; no shell commands or signaling in instruction content.

  3. WorkstreamRunner integration into Orchestrator. Separate PR (N½).

  5. Where do role templates live? src/agentrelay/templates/ with adapter-specific overrides in templates/<adapter_name>/.

  6. Protocol file format. Per AGENT_COMM_PROTOCOL.md: manifest.json (Layer 1), instructions.md (Layer 2), policies.json (Layer 3).
